reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware reasoning about which thermal-runaway progression claims are admissible under coupled conditions (high SOC, cold-cycled aging, high anode surface area), with correct options reflecting separable SOC- vs cycle-driven dependencies. Decoys are mostly single-fault and engineering-plausible (misattributed mechanisms, overclaimed rate limitation, minimized contributor), providing good expert discrimination. Minor weakness is stylistic/structural cueing: some options use absolute language (“rate-limiting,” “negligibly,” “primarily”) and the stem bundles many factors, which can slightly bias elimination and reduce neutrality.

The prompt cleanly targets a single mechanistic boundary: SEI-decomposition heat being surface-area-controlled vs post-SEI anode reactions being lithium/SOC-controlled, and asks the solver to localize the engineer’s uniform-SOC scaling mistake. The structure strongly rewards boundary-aware reasoning and penalizes overgeneralization, though as a short-answer item it lacks explicit decoys to audit for single-fault purity and may allow some variability in how respondents name/partition the >120°C processes while still sounding plausible.
